name: org.machanism.machai:ghostwriter
description: Ghostwriter is a Java library and CLI application in the Machai ecosystem for AI-assisted repository automation, guided documentation maintenance, and act-driven project workflows. Based on the generated Javadoc for Ghostwriter 1.1.15, the project provides command-line execution, reusable act processing, file-system tools, web access tools, command execution tools, and project-aware reviewer extension points. It is designed for scenarios where instructions are embedded in project files or encapsulated in reusable Act templates, allowing software teams to automate documentation generation, repository maintenance, and guided transformations while keeping prompts and rules close to the codebase. The library exposes processor classes for act execution and guided processing, SPI-based reviewer support for multiple file types, and host-integrated tool sets for reading and writing files, listing project structure, fetching web content, invoking REST APIs, and running controlled shell commands. This makes Ghostwriter suitable for developer tooling, AI-enabled software engineering automation, documentation pipelines, codebase governance, and CI/CD-adjacent workflows where a Java runtime and a compatible GenAI provider are available.

usageContext: Embedding Ghostwriter in Java applications to automate repository tasks
Running guided AI workflows from a command-line interface
Executing reusable Act templates for project maintenance
Extending supported file analysis via Reviewer SPI implementations
Building AI-assisted documentation and automation pipelines in CI/CD or local developer environments

constructors.description: Creates an ActProcessor that runs Ghostwriter in Act mode. The processor uses the supplied project root as the base for relative paths, a configurator as the configuration source, and a GenAI provider key or model identifier to execute prompt-driven workflows against project files.

examples.snippet:
org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator configurator = null;
org.machanism.machai.gw.processor.ActProcessor processor = new org.machanism.machai.gw.processor.ActProcessor(new java.io.File("."), configurator, "OpenAI:gpt-5.1");
examples.description: Creates an Act processor for the current project directory. This is the basic setup when embedding Ghostwriter into a Java application that wants to run reusable Act templates against repository files.

constructors.description: Creates the tool installer responsible for registering command execution, log inspection, task completion, and process termination functions into a GenAI provider. It is useful when embedding Ghostwriter tool capabilities into a host-integrated runtime.

examples.description: Instantiates the command tool set so it can later be attached to a provider and used to execute validated shell commands from Ghostwriter-driven workflows.

constructors.description: Creates the file-system tool installer that exposes file reading, writing, directory listing, and recursive file discovery operations to the runtime. This is one of the core building blocks for project-aware automation with Ghostwriter.

examples.description: Creates the file tool set used to provide safe file-system access to Ghostwriter-driven automations and host applications.

features.name: main
features.signature: public static void main(java.lang.String[] args) throws java.io.IOException, org.apache.commons.cli.ParseException
features.description: Starts the Ghostwriter CLI. This is the public entry point for running Ghostwriter as a standalone command-line application, making it possible to invoke repository automation and guided processing from scripts, terminals, or CI jobs.

examples.description: Illustrates a direct Java invocation of the CLI entry point. In practice, this method is typically executed through the packaged application rather than called manually, but it demonstrates how the CLI can be launched programmatically.

features.name: setAct
features.signature: public void setAct(java.lang.String act) throws java.io.IOException
features.description: Loads an act definition and applies it as the current execution defaults. The act string may include the act name followed by optional prompt text, allowing a host application to choose a reusable workflow and inject user input into the act template before scanning and processing files.

examples.snippet:
org.machanism.machai.gw.processor.ActProcessor processor = new org.machanism.machai.gw.processor.ActProcessor(new java.io.File("."), null, "OpenAI:gpt-5.1");
processor.setAct("task Rewrite the README for new users");
examples.description: Shows how to select an Act template and provide a practical request string. This is a common pattern when building a guided workflow launcher around Ghostwriter.

features.name: loadAct
features.signature: public static void loadAct(java.lang.String name, java.util.Map<java.lang.String, java.lang.Object> properties, java.lang.String actsLocation) throws java.io.IOException
features.description: Loads an Act definition into a property map while supporting inheritance through the basedOn property. The method can combine built-in acts with user-defined overrides from an external acts directory, enabling extension and customization of reusable workflows.

examples.snippet:
java.util.Map<java.lang.String, java.lang.Object> properties = new java.util.HashMap<java.lang.String, java.lang.Object>();
org.machanism.machai.gw.processor.ActProcessor.loadAct("help", properties, "./acts");
examples.description: Demonstrates how to inspect or pre-load an Act definition before execution. This is useful when building tooling that needs to validate, display, or customize available acts.

features.name: perform
features.signature: java.lang.String perform(java.io.File projectDir, java.io.File file) throws java.io.IOException
features.description: Reviews a file and returns a formatted fragment for the Ghostwriter pipeline. Implementations such as HtmlReviewer, JavaReviewer, MarkdownReviewer, PumlReviewer, PythonReviewer, TextReviewer, and TypeScriptReviewer interpret file-specific syntax to extract embedded guidance and related context.

examples.snippet:
org.machanism.machai.gw.reviewer.Reviewer reviewer = new org.machanism.machai.gw.reviewer.MarkdownReviewer();
java.lang.String result = reviewer.perform(new java.io.File("."), new java.io.File("README.md"));
examples.description: Demonstrates a practical how-to-use scenario in which a host application directly invokes a reviewer implementation to extract guidance from a Markdown file before deciding how to process it further.

features.name: getWebContent
features.description: Retrieves web content over HTTP GET and optionally extracts text by CSS selector. This feature supports web-assisted workflows where Ghostwriter needs documentation pages, remote resources, or selected fragments of HTML content as part of a prompt-driven process.

examples.description: In a host-integrated runtime, this tool enables practical retrieval of targeted web documentation or API reference snippets that can be passed into an AI workflow.

features.name: executeCommand
features.signature: public java.lang.String executeCommand(com.fasterxml.jackson.databind.JsonNode props, java.io.File projectDir) throws java.io.IOException
features.description: Executes a validated system command using Java ProcessBuilder and returns bounded output. This capability allows Ghostwriter-driven tasks to invoke local shell commands, inspect results, and combine command output with AI-assisted repository workflows.

examples.description: In a real-world host integration, this feature is used to run controlled shell commands such as directory listings, build commands, or diagnostics as part of a larger Ghostwriter automation flow.

customizations.name: Reviewer
customizations.description: Primary extension point for adding support for new file formats. Implement this interface to teach Ghostwriter how to scan additional file types and extract guidance fragments for downstream processing.

examples.description: Shows how a developer can extend Ghostwriter with a custom reviewer implementation for JSON files. This is a practical path for adapting Ghostwriter to repository-specific formats.

customizations.name: setActsLocation
customizations.description: Customization point for selecting the directory that contains external Act definition files. This allows teams to maintain project-specific or organization-specific workflows outside the built-in act set.

examples.description: Demonstrates how to point Ghostwriter at a custom acts directory so reusable workflows can be distributed and maintained separately from the built-in act catalog.

customizations.name: setDisableNormalOrder
customizations.description: Controls whether Ghostwriter continues with the default episode execution order after the requested episode is handled. This can be used to fine-tune multi-episode act behavior for custom workflow orchestration.

examples.description: Shows a practical customization where only explicitly requested episode behavior should run, which is useful in specialized integrations that manage episode transitions externally.

studs.name: org.machanism.machai.gw.reviewer.Reviewer
studs.description: Service-provider interface for components that scan project files and extract @guidance instructions for downstream processing. Implementations typically support a specific file format and produce normalized prompt fragments with path context.

examples.description: Illustrates how a host application can discover reviewer implementations and inspect their supported file extensions before running Ghostwriter-driven processing.

examples.title: Install Ghostwriter as a Maven dependency and configure a custom acts directory
examples.scenario: Embed Ghostwriter in a Java application that runs Act workflows against the current repository.
steps: 1. Add the Maven dependency org.machanism.machai:ghostwriter:1.1.15 to your project.
2. Ensure a Java 8+ runtime and a compatible GenAI provider identifier such as OpenAI:gpt-5.1 are available to your application.
3. Create an org.machanism.machai.gw.processor.ActProcessor with the project root directory, configurator, and provider name.
4. Call setActsLocation with a directory such as ./acts if you want custom Act definitions in addition to built-in acts.
5. Select an Act with setAct, for example task Rewrite the README for new users.
6. Run the surrounding workflow that scans files and processes the selected Act.
examples.description: This example demonstrates a practical library integration: install Ghostwriter as a dependency, configure an external acts directory, and choose a concrete workflow that can be applied to repository files.

examples.title: Run the Ghostwriter CLI from the command line
examples.scenario: Use Ghostwriter as a ready-to-run CLI application for repository automation.
steps: 1. Build or obtain the packaged Ghostwriter CLI distribution for version 1.1.15.
2. Make sure Java is installed and available on PATH.
3. Open a terminal in the target project directory.
4. Run the Ghostwriter CLI entry point using the packaged jar or launcher script.
5. Pass the project scan target and any required model or configuration arguments expected by your distribution.
6. Review the resulting generated or updated project files after the CLI completes.
examples.description: Practical how-to-use scenario for the standalone CLI. The Javadoc identifies org.machanism.machai.gw.processor.Ghostwriter.main(java.lang.String[] args) as the command-line entry point used to start Ghostwriter.

examples.title: Extend Ghostwriter with a custom Reviewer implementation
examples.scenario: Add support for a new repository file type so Ghostwriter can extract guidance from it.
steps: 1. Create a class that implements org.machanism.machai.gw.reviewer.Reviewer.
2. Implement getSupportedFileExtensions so Ghostwriter knows which file suffixes your reviewer handles.
3. Implement perform(java.io.File projectDir, java.io.File file) to read the file and extract any guidance fragment you want to return.
4. Register the implementation with Java ServiceLoader metadata in your build.
5. Package the extension so it is available on the application classpath.
6. Run Ghostwriter and verify that files with the new extension are routed through your custom reviewer.
examples.description: This example shows a real extension workflow for adapting Ghostwriter to new file formats. It is especially useful in organizations that embed repository-specific instructions in non-standard file types.

examples.title: Use Ghostwriter web and command tools in a host-integrated workflow
examples.scenario: Combine web retrieval and shell command execution as part of an AI-assisted automation task.
steps: 1. Instantiate org.machanism.machai.gw.tools.WebFunctionTools and org.machanism.machai.gw.tools.CommandFunctionTools in the host application.
2. Register these tool sets with the GenAI provider used by the workflow.
3. Use the web tool to fetch documentation or API content needed for context.
4. Use the command tool to run a validated local shell command such as a directory listing or build command.
5. Feed the collected results into the surrounding Ghostwriter prompt or Act execution.
6. Review the produced output and any modified repository files.
examples.description: Practical how-to-use scenario for advanced integrations where Ghostwriter needs both remote reference material and local command output to complete repository automation tasks.
